meta.title: Security Added To CIP / EtherNet/IP Protocol
meta.description:
For decades ICS protocols have lacked even those most basic security features such as authentication. Jack Visoky shows how this is changing with the ODVA adding security to CIP (EtherNet/IP is part of CIP).

They decided to use TLS and DTLS to wrap the protocol, and used it in a "pure and unmodified" form. The idea is CIP Security would leverage the tested and accepted security of TLS. (Note there was a debate session at S4x18 on whether wrap it in TLS is the right approach for ICS).

All endpoints be deployed with a default certificate, most likely signed by the vendor. And there are a variety of push and pull models that Jack goes over some of these. There are some less secure certificate management that are allowed such as self-signed certificates and trust on first use.

Jack finishes the session discussing proposed features in the next version of CIP Security. The ICS authorization, think passing role information, is intriguing.
